headline: First-ever evidence-based baseline (Gen-2 cluster forensic anchor): SCOTUS petition March 21, 2026 to declare FCA qui tam whistleblower provisions unconstitutional — explicit corporate intent to eliminate the federal accountability mechanism that historically returned $61M ($183M trebled) on Streck Medicaid-rebate case
methodology_notes: F500 Gen-2 cluster 4×7+3.5×1=81.4 — sixth consecutive forensic anchor invalidating this cluster. Distinct profile from Danaher and BD: Eli Lilly retains higher SYS (68.8) and ACT (68.8) scores reflecting genuine therapeutic-access mission (insulin manufacturing scale, GLP-1 Mounjaro/Zepbound supply ramping, oncology pipeline, donations programs). However, ACC drops sharply to 37.5 and INT to 43.8 because the SCOTUS petition is not merely defending a single case — it is explicitly seeking elimination of the federal whistleblower/qui-tam mechanism itself. Per Palantir-2026-04-23 precedent on values-divergence (corporate manifestos vs. verified practices), an entity's affirmative legal action to dismantle accountability infrastructure is direct INT-dimension evidence regardless of the petition's success. The underlying $183M Streck judgment (Medicaid rebate misreporting, jury 2022, treble damages affirmed by 7th Cir.) is Tier-1 court-record evidence of misreporting. EQU at 50.0 reflects ongoing healthcare access concerns (insulin pricing class actions, GLP-1 access disparities). Note: Eli Lilly's published scores match the F500 Gen-2 4×7+3.5×1 cluster precisely — invalidation of cluster by BD-Danaher anchors triggers this baseline. Band change Exemplary → Functional consistent with calibration.

evidence.claim: Eli Lilly filed certiorari petition March 21, 2026 (No. 25-1126) asking SCOTUS to rule FCA qui tam provisions unconstitutional under Article II's Vesting Clause

Underlying Streck v. Lilly judgment: 7th Circuit affirmed $61M jury verdict (trebled to ~$183M under FCA) for misreporting Medicaid Average Manufacturer Price; Lilly's certiorari attempt seeks to invalidate the entire qui tam mechanism rather than appeal facts
Lilly arguments characterized as targeting 'private bounty hunters' and seeking fee-shifting reform — reframes whistleblower mechanism as illegitimate
D&O Diary legal analysis April 2026: petition has potential industry-wide implications; framing as 'corporate intent to eliminate qui tam' rather than narrow appeal
Lilly's healthcare mission infrastructure (insulin manufacturing scale, Mounjaro/Zepbound diabetes/obesity supply, cancer pipeline, LLY Foundation access programs) supports SYS/ACT 68.8 floor
Published 81.4 is uniform 4×7+3.5×1 Gen-2 cluster profile — fourth consecutive forensic invalidation by BD, Danaher, and General Mills anchors in 2-day window

key_evidence: SCOTUS certiorari petition March 21, 2026 — explicit corporate intent to eliminate FCA qui tam whistleblower mechanism as unconstitutional
Underlying $183M Streck judgment for Medicaid Average Manufacturer Price misreporting (7th Cir. affirmed)
Per Palantir-2026-04-23 precedent: affirmative legal action to dismantle accountability infrastructure is Tier-1 INT/ACC evidence regardless of petition outcome
ACC 37.5 and INT 43.8 are the lowest scores; SYS 68.8 and ACT 68.8 retained on therapeutic-access mission
Published 81.4 is uniform Gen-2 cluster profile invalidated by BD-Danaher-GM anchors
